---
title: "Project structure"
description: "How namespaces, configs, and the target/ build map together."
order: 10
---

## Repository layout {#sec-project-structure}

The root of the repository contains two main folders:

1. `src` — the source code for components and workflows.
2. `target` — build outputs (only present after components have been built).

Each subfolder of `src` is a Viash
[namespace](https://viash.io/guides/projects/namespaces/): a logical grouping of
components with similar function. Within a namespace, each subfolder is one
component. For example `src/convert/from_bdrhap_to_h5mu` implements the
`from_bdrhap_to_h5mu` component, grouped with others such as
`from_10xmtx_to_h5mu` into the `convert` namespace. Pipelines are grouped into
folders in the same way, but these are not component namespaces and do not
interact with `viash ns` commands.

Viash provides commands that operate on a single component, on a whole namespace,
or on the entire project. As a rule of thumb, the basic commands (like
`viash test`) act on individual components, while `ns` commands (like
`viash ns test`) act on namespaces. A fresh clone has no `target` folder — it is
created only after components are built.

## Versioning and branching {#sec-versioning}

OpenPipeline follows [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/): releases use a
`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` number. At major version 4.x, public-facing breaking changes
are documented in a dedicated section of the CHANGELOG on each `MINOR` release. A
`PATCH` release resolves bugs without introducing breaking changes — though a
patch may correct behavior that was itself an inadvertent earlier breaking change.

Between releases, development happens on Git branches. All pull requests target
the **`main`** branch, so `main` holds the latest code and acts as the
development branch. Once a pull request is approved and merged, GitHub Actions CI
builds all components (creating `target/`) and pushes the result to the
**`main_build`** branch — a copy of `main` that also contains the built
components.

To cut a release, the CI release workflow is triggered manually: components on
`main` are built and tested, the result is pushed to the **`release`** branch,
and integration tests run. If they pass, a GitHub tag and release are created
from `release`. A release also publishes the auto-generated reference
documentation.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: { 'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'default'} } }%%
gitGraph
  commit id: "initial commit"
  branch main_build
  commit id: "CI build"
  checkout main
  commit
  checkout main_build
  merge main
  checkout main
  branch feature_a
  branch feature_b
  checkout feature_a
  commit
  commit
  checkout main
  commit id: "#release 0.1" type: HIGHLIGHT
  checkout main_build
  merge main
  checkout main
  branch release
  commit tag: "0.1"
  checkout main
  commit
  checkout feature_b
  commit
  commit
  checkout feature_a
  commit
  checkout main
  merge feature_a
  checkout main_build
  merge main
  checkout main
  checkout feature_b
  commit
  checkout main
  merge feature_b
  checkout main_build
  merge main
  checkout release
  merge main tag: "0.2"
```

**Next:** [Creating components →](creating-components.qmd)
